# Copilot instructions — SerialCommunication

Purpose
- Short guidance for Copilot sessions working on this repository (Windows Forms + Arduino serial example).

Quick commands
- Build (Windows/CI): use Visual Studio 2017+ or MSBuild (Developer Command Prompt):
  - msbuild SerialCommunication.slnx /p:Configuration=Debug
  - msbuild SerialCommunication\SerialCommunication.csproj /p:Configuration=Release
- Run locally: open SerialCommunication.slnx in Visual Studio and press F5. Binary appears in SerialCommunication\bin\Debug\SerialCommunication.exe.
- Tests: none present in repository.
- Lint: no linter configured.
- Arduino sketch: open arduino\SerialCommunication\SerialCommunication.ino in the Arduino IDE, select board/port and Upload. Sketch uses Baudrate 115200.

High-level architecture
- Desktop app: SerialCommunication (C# .NET Framework 4.7.2) — a single WinForms application (Form1) that enumerates serial ports, exposes port/baud/parity/handshake settings, and provides multiple "exercise" tabs (Oefening1..5) representing UI controls to manipulate pins.
- Embedded side: arduino/SerialCommunication — an Arduino sketch plus a bundled SerialCommand parser (SerialCommand.h/.cpp and analog helper). The sketch exposes simple ASCII commands: set, toggle, get, ping, help, debug.
- Integration: the WinForms app is intended to talk to the Arduino using newline-terminated ASCII commands at 115200 baud. The Arduino parses tokens separated by spaces and executes pin operations.

Key conventions and repository specifics
- Language & labels: many UI identifiers and labels are Dutch (e.g., "Poort", "Instellingen", "Oefening"). Search the codebase for Dutch terms when looking for UI elements.
- Designer vs code: Form UI is generated by Form1.Designer.cs. Avoid hand-editing the Designer region; update the UI via the Visual Studio Designer and keep code in Form1.cs.
- Serial protocol details (Arduino):
  - Terminator: newline (\n) / term variable set in SerialCommand.
  - Buffer and limits: SERIALCOMMANDBUFFER = 32, MAXSERIALCOMMANDS = 10. Commands larger than the buffer or more handlers than max will be ignored or wrapped.
  - Command format examples (from sketch):
    - set d2 on
    - set pwm9 128
    - toggle d3
    - get d2
    - get a0
    - ping
  - Pin mapping used in sketch: digital outputs D2..D4, PWM outputs D9..D11, digital inputs D5..D7, analog A0..A5.
- Project structure: single Visual Studio solution (SerialCommunication.slnx) with one WinForms project and an Arduino folder containing the embedded firmware. Resources (images) for the UI live under SerialCommunication\Resources.
- Partial/incomplete desktop implementation: the connect button handler (buttonConnect_Click) is currently empty; Copilot should prefer making minimal, testable changes (e.g., implement serial open/close, then wire a single command action) and avoid refactoring designer-generated code.

Files to inspect first (for quick context)
- SerialCommunication\SerialCommunication.csproj
- SerialCommunication\Form1.cs
- SerialCommunication\Form1.Designer.cs
- arduino\SerialCommunication\SerialCommunication.ino
- arduino\SerialCommunication\SerialCommand.h/.cpp

Existing AI assistant configs discovered
- None of the common assistant config files (CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md, .cursorrules, .windsurfrules, CONVENTIONS.md, etc.) were found.

Suggested Copilot prompts
- "Add serial connect/disconnect logic to Form1.buttonConnect_Click using System.IO.Ports.SerialPort. Use the selected port and baudrate from combo boxes and update radioButtonVerbonden to show connection state. Keep the Designer code unchanged."
- "Implement a helper that maps UI actions to Arduino commands (e.g., set d2 on) and send them over an open SerialPort. Add minimal error handling and a simple console/status output."

Maintaining changes
- Prefer small, testable commits. Keep UI changes in Designer via Visual Studio to avoid merging conflicts.

If anything important was missed or needs more detail (CI, testing, or wiring instructions), reply asking for that specific addition.
